content:
Augmented Reality

Web Development

JavaScript

Virtual Try On

Sdk

A practical guide to Web AR APIs in 2026: what breaks when you DIY, what to look for in an SDK, and how to get from zero to working try-on in a week.

7 min read
1 hour ago

--

TL;DR
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Bottom line: If your use case involves face tracking for virtual try-on, beauty, or eyewear, the DIY path takes quarters and produces worse tracking than an SDK you can license for a fraction of the engineering cost. I learned this the hard way.

Why I stopped building it myself
A couple of years ago I started building a Web AR prototype for a client. Beauty try-on in the browser, no app install. The pitch was simple. The build was not.
Here is what the “simple” version actually required: WebRTC camera capture with permission handling that behaves differently across iOS Safari and Android Chrome, a face landmark model that doesn’t jitter when someone moves their head, background segmentation that doesn’t make hair look like it’s on fire, a WebGL renderer for the effects, and an effect format that a designer could actually work with without writing GLSL shaders.
I got it working. Six months later. On a narrow set of devices. With tracking that was fine on a Pixel 7 and embarrassing on a mid-range Xiaomi.
The client launched on schedule only because we switched to an SDK three months in. The lesson was expensive enough that I don’t repeat it.

Three things that actually break Web AR at production scale
Before getting into what to look for in an API, here are the failure modes that don’t show up in demos.
1. Tracking jitter
Eyes drift, glasses slide off the nose during a head turn, lipstick bleeds past the lip line. In a virtual try-on context, this is not a minor annoyance. It is the thing that kills conversion. Users see the effect misalign and immediately distrust the product preview. I’ve seen this tank a well-designed try-on experience that had good UX otherwise.
2. Performance collapse on real hardware
A demo that runs at 30 FPS on a MacBook Pro drops to 12 FPS on a 2021 mid-range Android. That is the device a significant portion of your users have. The face landmark models you find on TensorFlow.js Hub are not optimized for this. You end up hand-rolling WebAssembly optimizations that are basically the same work the SDK vendor already did.
3. Browser fragmentation
iOS Safari handles MediaStream constraints differently than Chrome. Android WebRTC has its own quirks. WebAssembly SIMD support is still uneven. If you build it yourself, you own this permanently. Every browser update is a potential regression. The vendor path means someone else watches for this.

What a production Web AR app actually needs
Here is the capability checklist I use when evaluating either a DIY build or a vendor. If a vendor can’t check all of these, find out why before committing.
Camera and input
WebRTC webcam capture with robust permission handling, frame normalization that survives platform-specific webcam orientation quirks, support for image, video file, and external MediaStream inputs.
Face tracking
Real-time landmark detection with 3D face mesh reconstruction, not just 2D point detection. 2D landmark models lose stability the moment someone turns their head past 30 degrees. 3D mesh tracking holds.
Segmentation
Background, hair, lips, and skin segmentation as separate modules. Not all experiences need all of these, but you want them available without rebuilding the stack.
Rendering
WebGL pipeline with an effect format that a designer can work with. If the only way to create a new effect is to write shaders, you will become the bottleneck for every content update.
Output
Photo capture, video recording, MediaStream export for WebRTC integrations. This last one matters if the Web AR feed needs to go into a video call or live stream.

The DIY path: what it actually costs
The standard stack for rolling your own Web AR: TensorFlow.js or MediaPipe for face landmarks, Three.js or Babylon.js for rendering, WebXR Device API for markerless tracking on Android Chrome, custom WebAssembly modules for performance-critical inference, and something homebrew for an effect format.
The pros are real. You own the IP, you can tune every model, no licensing fees.
The cons are why most teams abandon this path within a quarter:

• Time to feature parity with a commercial SDK: six to twelve months for a serious team. I’m not being pessimistic. This is the range I’ve seen across projects.

• Tracking quality under real conditions. Off-the-shelf face landmark models from MediaPipe are good. They are not “lipstick stays on the lip line when someone talks” good. That level of tracking requires fine-tuned models and an occlusion handling layer that takes real research time to get right.

• Cross-browser maintenance becomes a permanent tax. Safari and Chrome diverge in ways that require ongoing attention. This is not a one-time setup cost.
When DIY makes sense:
AR is your actual core product, you have a computer vision research team, and you have a multi-year timeline. For everyone else, the math doesn’t work.

Using Banuba Web AR SDK: how it actually integrates
Banuba’s Web AR SDK ships as the @banuba/webar npm package. It handles the WebAssembly loading, GPU rendering, ML inference, and DOM output. The JavaScript API is straightforward.
Install
npm install @banuba/webarBasic integration
import { Player, Effect, ImageCapture } from '@banuba/webar'const player = await Player.create({
clientToken: 'YOUR_CLIENT_TOKEN',
// Target DOM element where the AR output renders
target: document.getElementById('webar-container')
})// Attach webcam input
await player.setCameraStream(
await navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({
video: { width: 1280, height: 720, facingMode: 'user' }
})
)// Load and apply an effect
const effect = new Effect('effects/Makeup_Lipstick_Red.zip')
await player.applyEffect(effect)// Start rendering
await player.play()Switch effects at runtime (for try-on catalog)
// User selects a different shade from the catalog
async function applyShade(effectPath) {
const effect = new Effect(effectPath)
await player.applyEffect(effect)
}// Example: lipstick shade switcher
document.querySelectorAll('.shade-swatch').forEach(swatch => {
swatch.addEventListener('click', () => {
applyShade(swatch.dataset.effectPath)
})
})Photo capture
// Capture the current AR frame as a downloadable image
const capture = new ImageCapture(player)
const blob = await capture.takePhoto()const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob)
const link = document.createElement('a')
link.href = url
link.download = 'try-on.jpg'
link.click()React integration
import { useEffect, useRef } from 'react'
import { Player, Effect } from '@banuba/webar'export function WebARCamera({ effectPath }: { effectPath: string }) {
const containerRef = useRef<HTMLDivElement>(null)
const playerRef = useRef<Player | null>(null) useEffect(() => {
let player: Player async function init() {
player = await Player.create({
clientToken: process.env.REACT_APP_BANUBA_TOKEN!,
target: containerRef.current!
}) const stream = await navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia({
video: { facingMode: 'user' }
})
await player.setCameraStream(stream)
await player.applyEffect(new Effect(effectPath))
await player.play()
playerRef.current = player
} init() return () => {
player?.stop()
}
}, [effectPath]) return <div ref={containerRef} style={{ width: '100%', height: '100%' }} />
}What the SDK replaces in your stack
The engine runs two WebAssembly binaries: BanubaSDK.wasm for broad compatibility and BanubaSDK.simd.wasm for browsers with SIMD support, which handles heavier inference faster. You don't manage which one loads: the SDK picks based on browser capability.
Segmentation modules (background, hair, lips) are loaded separately, so you only ship the perception your experience actually needs.
The effect format is produced in Banuba Studio, a browser-based editor that lets designers build filters and makeup looks without writing shaders. This is the thing that made the most difference on client projects: content updates stopped going through the engineering queue.

Performance I actually measured
Testing on a 2021 Samsung Galaxy A32 (Snapdragon 720G, a representative mid-range Android):
Press enter or click to view image in full size

30 FPS is the floor where the experience feels live rather than choppy. The basic try-on use cases stay above it on mid-range hardware. The heavier 3D effects push toward the edge, which is worth knowing before you plan a campaign around complex masks.

How to decide: four questions

• Is AR the core product or a feature?
Core means the DIY path deserves serious consideration. Feature means use an SDK and spend the engineering cycles on something that differentiates you.

• What is the timeline?
Anything under three months points to an API. Three months is not enough to build stable cross-browser face tracking from scratch.

• What tracking quality do you need?
Virtual try-on, eyewear, and makeup require 3D face mesh tracking that holds up under occlusion. This is genuinely hard to build and straightforward to license.

• Who maintains it next year?
If you don’t have a dedicated team to chase browser updates and ML regressions, the vendor path is lower risk. This question eliminates a lot of DIY plans in the second year.

What’s changed in 2026 specifically
Two things shifted the WebAR landscape enough to be worth naming.
8th Wall went open source in February 2026. The tracking tech that used to sit behind an enterprise license is now accessible to anyone. It’s primarily strong for marker-based and scene AR. For face tracking specifically, it’s less relevant, but for teams building spatial AR in a browser, this is significant.
WebXR is now stable in Chrome, Edge, and Samsung Internet, with growing Safari support. The browser fragmentation problem hasn’t disappeared, but it’s smaller than it was two years ago.
WebAssembly SIMD support is now broad enough to make the heavier inference work viable in browsers without a dedicated GPU. This changes the performance floor on devices that were previously too slow for real-time face AR in the browser.
The net effect of all three is that the reasons I was skeptical about WebAR two years ago are mostly smaller now. Browser support is more consistent. The performance floor is higher. The open-source ecosystem is richer.
What hasn’t changed is the time cost of building the face tracking layer from scratch. That’s still the part that takes quarters, not weeks. And for most teams building try-on or beauty features in a browser, that’s the only calculation that matters.
If you’re scoping a Web AR build right now: start with the SDK, ship something real, and revisit the in-house question once you have conversion data. The data will tell you whether custom tracking is worth the investment. It usually isn’t.
